"I can provide the contract reference numbers," Cross said. "From memory. They're in the format I reviewed three years ago. I don't have the full details but I have enough for Elara to request the relevant archive access through the Council's evidence protocol."

He provided them. Lian wrote them down and included them in the message. The relay chain was running.

Iris opened her notebook and began recording Cross's account of the founding of the Erasure Exchange — not because it was in the registry's scope (the registry recorded the erased, not the institutional history of the erasure apparatus) but because it was the kind of information that was more dangerous to leave undocumented than to document. The specific pattern of Sola's folder, of Sect's authorization chain, of Cross's Record chamber testimony: information that had been circulating in the Archive's internal systems for decades, classified piecemeal, the full picture invisible to anyone whose access was limited to their own division's records.

Cross was the first person who had had access to both the calibration division's records and the external affairs division's procurement records and the Phase III team's development files. Not because the Archive had intended him to have all three. Because a fifteen-year career in a Senior role in the calibration wing had given him incidental cross-division access through fifteen years of resource reviews and coordination meetings and the specific information drift that happened in any large institution when personnel were assigned to cross-divisional projects and their access permissions were updated for the project and then not revoked afterward.

The Archive had spent forty-four years building an information silo structure that prevented anyone from seeing the full picture. Valen Cross had been inside the silo long enough that the walls had developed gaps.

Corin looked at the Citadel's walls.

Inside those walls: the sub-basement, the locked storage room with three hundred kilograms of array components. The maintenance tunnel's eastern terminus that Cross had found fifteen years ago and had never documented. The service corridor, the station walk, the nine spiral chambers, the ninth chamber with its copper Fibonacci spiral and the twelve seconds of partial stabilization still present in the entity's substrate. Arcwright, preparing to testify.

Eighteen hours until Arcwright's testimony. Twenty until the deliberation window closed. Cross's credentials still active.

"How do we get to the maintenance tunnel terminus?" Corin said.

Cross told him.

The approach to the inner perimeter began.

His body — the body he had last inhabited seventeen years ago, before the erasure order, before Arcwright's contract, before the anchor embedded his consciousness into the Resonance — was whole. The entity's consent had restored him. The same consent that sustained the erased and the pre-collapse returnees. The same consent that had been asked, for the first time in eight centuries, by his son.

Lira was the first person he saw. She was older than he remembered — twelve years in the Rift, five months of continuous transmission, the silver blood that had finally stopped bleeding when the nerves died. Her hair was gray now, cut short and practical, the same dark brown as Corin's visible only at the roots. But her eyes — the storm-colored irises, the most powerful Chronometric sensitivity ever recorded — were the same as they had been seventeen years ago. And she was looking at him with an expression that contained twelve years of imprisonment and five months of restoration and seventeen years of waiting for her husband to come back.

"You're late," she said. Her voice was steady — the same calm she had used in Cell 17, in the ninth chamber, in the sub-basement. But her hand — the hand that had been bleeding silver for five months — was gripping his with a force that made his knuckles ache.

"I know." His voice — the voice Corin had heard in the Resonance Anchor, in the tuning den in Harrow, in the Echo that had shattered into six simultaneous deaths — was the same. Tired. Certain. The voice of a man who had died fourteen times across a continuous erasure and had kept his consciousness intact by embedding it into the frequency that was now sustaining the restoration. "Seventeen years. I counted every one."

"The anchor preserved you. But the world — "

"I know. Lira told me. Through the symphony. Everything. The Rift. The ninth chamber. The entity. The restoration. The network." He looked at Corin — the connecting thread, the apprentice whose Echo had broken, the boy who had carried seventeen years of his father's research across three hundred kilometers of Unwoven territory and five months of impossible choices. "You did everything I hoped you would do. And more than I ever imagined."

"I had help."

"Everyone has help. The difference — the thing that made the restoration possible, the thing that made the entity consent, the thing that made Arcwright surrender and Vessa convert and Joren witness — is that you asked for it. You didn't try to do it alone. You didn't try to be the hero. You were the receiver. The connecting thread. The person who tied together everyone who had been working toward this moment. That's not help. That's leadership. The kind the Archive never understood — because the Archive thought leadership was control. You proved it was connection."

Corin felt the weight of the data crystal in his pocket — seventeen years of research, seventeen years of waiting, seventeen years of his father's consciousness preserved in an anchor that had been designed to survive erasures. The weight was still there. It would always be there — the accumulated knowledge of a man who had discovered the entity's pain response and mapped its frequencies and designed the array that was now sustaining the restoration. But the weight was no longer his alone.

"Welcome back," he said. "We have a lot to catch you up on."

Aldric Draven looked at his son — the connecting thread — and smiled. "Start from the beginning. I have seventeen years to make up for."
